title: Anti-GM1 ganglioside igm-antibodies present in human plasma: Affinity and biological activity changes in a patient with neuropathy
abstract:
Anti-GM1 Ganglioside IgM-Antibodies Present
in Human Plasma: Affinity and Biological
Activity Changes in a Patient With Neuropathy
Roxana K. Mizutamari,1 Leonardo J. Kremer,2 Elizabeth A. Basile,2 and Gustavo A. Nores1*
1Departamento de Quı´mica Biolo´gica ‘‘Dr. Ranwel Caputto,’’ Facultad de Ciencias Quı´micas,
Universidad Nacional de Co´rdoba and CIQUIBIC, CONICET, Co´rdoba, Argentina
2Instituto de Neurociencias, Co´rdoba, Argentina
Low affinity anti-GM1 IgM-antibodies are part of the
normal repertoire of human plasma antibodies (Mizu-
tamari et al.: J Neuroimmunol 50:215–220, 1994), a
fact that is against the pathological role proposed for
them in autoimmune diseases. Here we present evi-
dence that these low affinity IgM-antibodies are de-
void of complement-mediated lytic activity to GM1-
liposomes, suggesting that they should not be
considered harmful. In contrast to the absence in
normal individuals, in the plasma of a patient with
sensory polyneuropathy we detected high affinity
anti-GM1 IgM-antibodies. Concomitant with the pres-
ence of these high affinity anti-GM1 IgM-antibodies,
the patient plasma is capable of producing comple-
ment-mediated lysis of GM1-liposomes. These results
suggest that an increase in the affinity of the naturally
existing anti-GM1 antibodies could be the trigger that
switches them from non-harmful to pathological. J.
Neurosci. Res. 51:237–242, 1998.
r 1998 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: gangliosides; GM1; anti-GM1 antibodies;
neuropathy
INTRODUCTION
Since the identiﬁcation of gangliosides as antigens
for ‘‘M-proteins’’ present in patients with neuropathy
associated with gammopathy (Ilyas et al., 1985; Nardelli
et al., 1987; Latov et al., 1988), the interest to study the
pathological role of anti-ganglioside antibodies has been
growing. Although a clear association between high titer
of polyclonal IgM anti-ganglioside antibodies and motor
neuron disease or neuropathies (Nobile-Orazio et al.,
1990; Quarles et al., 1990; Salazar-Grueso et al., 1990;
Nobile-Orazio et al., 1992) has been found, both the
etiological and clinical signiﬁcance of these antibodies
have not been established in a conclusive form. The high
frequency of low titer of anti-ganglioside antibodies in
neurological and non-neurological disease and some
normal controls (Endo et al., 1984; Nobile-Orazio et al.,
1992) contributes to the complexity of this problem.
Anti-ganglioside IgM-antibodies (mainly anti-GM1) are
part of the normal antibody repertoire of man (Mizuta-
mari et al., 1994), rabbit, and mouse (Mizutamari and
Nores, unpublished). The presence of these antibodies in
healthy individuals is against a pathological role pro-
posed for them in patients unless differences in their
quality as antibodies are shown that could be responsible
for their pathogenesis. We found that normal anti-GM1
antibodies are of low affinity and appear to be cross-
reacting antibodies originally directed to a GA1-like
structure (Mizutamari et al., 1994). Here we describe that
in a patient with sensory polyneuropathy a high affinity
anti-GM1 IgM-antibody population is present. Addition-
ally, we examine the biological activity of plasma from
healthy individuals and from a neuropathy patient by
determining their capacity to produce complement-
dependent lysis of ganglioside-containing liposomes.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Human Plasmas
A sample of plasma from a 35-year-old woman
showing symptoms of sensory polyneuropathy without
gammopathy was obtained previous to any treatment.
Normal human plasmas were provided by the Blood
Bank of the University of Co´rdoba, Argentina. All of the
donors were healthy adult volunteers with negative
serology for common infectious diseases. Sodium citrate
was used as anticoagulant. After blood cell separation
(usually less than 3 hr after extraction) the plasmas were
complement inactivated and frozen at 270°C until use.
Contract grant sponsor: CONICET; Contract grant number: PMT-PICT
0462; Contract grant sponsors: CONICOR and Secretarı´a de Ciencia y
Tecnica de la Universidad Nacional de Co´rdoba.
*Correspondence to: Dr. GustavoA. Nores, CIQUIBIC,Agencia Postal
4, Co´rdoba 5000, Argentina.
Received 28 June 1997; Revised 6 August 1997; Accepted 6 August
1997
Journal of Neuroscience Research 51:237–242 (1998)
r 1998 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
 Glycolipids
The glycolipids used in this study were puriﬁed
from the following sources: total gangliosides (mainly
GM1, GD1a, GD1b, and GT1b) from human brain, GM2
from Tay-Sachs brain, GM3 from dog erythrocytes, GM1
from calf brain, and LM1 from human erythrocytes. GA1
(asialo-GM1) was prepared by acid hydrolysis of cow
brain gangliosides (Cumar et al., 1968).
Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA)
The ELISA protocol was selected according to the
recommendations of the workshop ‘‘Measurement and
Signiﬁcance of Antibodies Against GM1 Ganglioside’’
(Marcus et al., 1989a). Twenty-ﬁve picomoles of glycolip-
ids in 50 µl of methanol was pipetted into microtiter plate
wells. After air drying, the wells were blocked with 1%
bovine serum albumin in phosphate-buffered saline (BSA-
PBS) for 1 hr. BSA-PBS diluted plasma or puriﬁed
antibodies (50 µl) was added to the well, incubated
overnight, and washed thoroughly with PBS. Binding
was detected following a 2 hr incubation period with
BSA-PBS diluted (1/1,000) peroxidase-conjugated rabbit
anti-human IgM-antibodies (Dakopatts, Hamburg, Ger-
many). All of the incubation steps were performed at 4°C.
After washing, color development was achieved in a
substrate solution containing 15 mM o-phenylenediamine
and 0.015% H2O2 in 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer, pH 5.0,
at room temperature. The reaction was stopped after 30
min by addition of 100 µl of 0.5 N H2SO4 and the optical
density (OD) was measured at 450 nm. Non-speciﬁc
antibody binding (OD value in a well not containing
glycolipid) was subtracted from each measurement. Titer,
deﬁned as the reciprocal of the plasma dilution required
to obtain the half-maximal antibody binding, is calculated
as Vl/VP0.5, where Vl is the incubation volume and VP0.5 is
the volume of plasma necessary to get the half-maximal
antibody binding. This value is determined using a double
reciprocal plot of the plasma volume added to the well vs.
the OD values (Mizutamari et al., 1994).
For the estimation of antibody binding affinity, in
some cases the wells were treated for 5 min with solutions
of potassium thiocyanate in PBS before adding the
secondary antibody.
Thin Layer Chromatographic (TLC)
Immunostaining
Aglycolipid library containing human brain ganglio-
sides (mainly GM1, GD1a, GD1b, and GT1b) with the
addition of 0.25 nmol each of GM3, GA1, GM2, and LM1
was separated on high performance TLC (HPTLC) plates
in the running solvent chloroform-methanol-aqueous 0.2%
CaCl2 (60:35:8) using a tank designed to obtain highly
reproducible chromatograms (Nores et al., 1994). After
air drying, the plates were coated by dipping for 1 min in
a 0.5% solution of polyisobutylmethacrylate (Plexigum P
28, Ro¨hm and Haas, Darmstadt, Germany) in n-hexane-
chloroform (9:1) and air dried for 10 min. The immuno-
staining protocol corresponds to that of ELISA except
that PBS contains 0.05% Tween 20 and the substrate
solution contains 2.8 mM 4-chloro-1-naphthol and 0.01%
H2O2 in methanol-20 mM Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 (1:29).
Antibody binding affinity was estimated by incubation of
the plates with 1 M potassium thiocyanate in PBS before
incubation with the secondary antibody.
Preparation of GM1 Affinity Column
The method used was essentially that of Hirabaya-
shi et al. (1983), which we previously found suitable for
the separation of anti-glycolipid antibody populations
(Nores et al., 1991; Mizutamari et al., 1994). Brieﬂy, 1.5
ml of methanol-aqueous 0.2 M KCl (1:1) containing 0.25
µmol of GM1 was added to 1 ml of octyl-Sepharose
(Sigma, St. Louis, MO). The mixture was rotated end
over end for 1 hr at room temperature. After removal of
the methanolic solution, the gel was washed 3 times with
5 volumes of PBS. The remaining steps were performed
at 4°C. Small portions of gel were loaded in calibrated
columns and washed with PBS. Human plasma (5 ml/0.1
ml of gel) was recycled through the column for 12 hr with
a peristaltic pump. The non-retained material does not
suffer any evident dilution and it is frozen as it is. After
washing the column with PBS and 0.5 M NaCl, the
retained antibodies were eluted with 1 M potassium
thiocyanate in BSA-PBS, desalted in Sephadex G-25,
diluted to the original plasma volume, and frozen at
270°C until use.
Complement-Dependent Liposome Lysis Assay
Liposome lysis assays were performed as previ-
ously described by Six et al. (1974), with special consid-
eration for the antigen density (Nores et al., 1987). Five
hundred nanomoles of dipalmitoyl-phosphatidyl choline,
500 nmol of cholesterol, 20 nmol of dicetylphosphate,
and variable quantities of GM1 or GA1 were dried and
resuspended in 0.1 ml of 100 mM umbelliferyl-phos-
phate. The resultant liposomes were washed 2 times with
TBS (20 mM Tris-HCl buffer, pH 8, 150 mM NaCl).
Twenty microliters of the liposome suspension was
incubated at room temperature with complement (30 µl)
and the stated amount of complement inactivated human
plasma or puriﬁed antibodies in a ﬁnal volume of 2 ml of
TBS containing 1.5 mM CaCl2 and 0.5 mM MgCl2. The
release of umbelliferyl-phosphate was measured, using
alkaline phosphatase, and quantiﬁcation of umbellifenone
was by ﬂuorescence. As the source of complement we
238
Mizutamari et al.

and 0.015% H2O2 in 0.1 M sodium acetate buffer, pH 5.0,
at room temperature. The reaction was stopped after 30

the OD values (Mizutamari et al., 1994).

Thin Layer Chromatographic (TLC)
Aglycolipid library containing human brain ganglio-
sides (mainly GM1, GD1a, GD1b, and GT1b) with the
addition of 0.25 nmol each of GM3, GA1, GM2, and LM1
was separated on high performance TLC (HPTLC) plates
in the running solvent chloroform-methanol-aqueous 0.2%
CaCl2 (60:35:8) using a tank designed to obtain highly
reproducible chromatograms (Nores et al., 1994). After

28, Ro¨hm and Haas, Darmstadt, Germany) in n-hexane-
chloroform (9:1) and air dried for 10 min. The immuno-
staining protocol corresponds to that of ELISA except
that PBS contains 0.05% Tween 20 and the substrate
solution contains 2.8 mM 4-chloro-1-naphthol and 0.01%
H2O2 in methanol-20 mM Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 (1:29).
Antibody binding affinity was estimated by incubation of
the plates with 1 M potassium thiocyanate in PBS before
incubation with the secondary antibody.
Preparation of GM1 Affinity Column

shi et al. (1983), which we previously found suitable for
the separation of anti-glycolipid antibody populations
(Nores et al., 1991; Mizutamari et al., 1994). Brieﬂy, 1.5
ml of methanol-aqueous 0.2 M KCl (1:1) containing 0.25
µmol of GM1 was added to 1 ml of octyl-Sepharose
(Sigma, St. Louis, MO). The mixture was rotated end

Complement-Dependent Liposome Lysis Assay
Liposome lysis assays were performed as previ-
ously described by Six et al. (1974), with special consid-
eration for the antigen density (Nores et al., 1987). Five
hundred nanomoles of dipalmitoyl-phosphatidyl choline,
500 nmol of cholesterol, 20 nmol of dicetylphosphate,
and variable quantities of GM1 or GA1 were dried and
resuspended in 0.1 ml of 100 mM umbelliferyl-phos-
phate. The resultant liposomes were washed 2 times with
TBS (20 mM Tris-HCl buffer, pH 8, 150 mM NaCl).
Twenty microliters of the liposome suspension was
incubated at room temperature with complement (30 µl)
and the stated amount of complement inactivated human
plasma or puriﬁed antibodies in a ﬁnal volume of 2 ml of
TBS containing 1.5 mM CaCl2 and 0.5 mM MgCl2. The
release of umbelliferyl-phosphate was measured, using
alkaline phosphatase, and quantiﬁcation of umbellifenone
was by ﬂuorescence. As the source of complement we
used human plasma depleted of anti-GM1/GA1 antibodies
by using an affinity column containing both glycolipids.
Results are expressed as percent of lysis considering as
100% the lysis obtained in the presence of 0.1% Triton
X-100. Spontaneous ﬂuorescence release (release in the
absence of antibodies) for the different liposome prepara-
tions is between 3 and 5% in 1 hr.
In preliminary experiments, the capability of nor-
mal human plasma to produce complement-dependent
lysis of ganglioside-containing liposomes was assayed.
Although the trapped marker was released in a time- and
complement-dependent way (results not shown), high
values of spontaneous lysis (release in the absence of
antibodies) was observed. This release is probably pro-
duced by the presence of anti-ganglioside antibodies in
the guinea pig serum used as the source of complement.
For this reason we used as the source of complement
human plasma that was passed through a GA1/GM1
affinity column until no GA1/GM1 HPTLC immunoreac-
tivity was detected. In this way, spontaneous release is
reduced to a value compatible with the experimental
times (3–5% in 1 hr). The lysis of GM1-liposomes by
human plasma shows ‘‘density dependence’’(Fig. 1). The
liposomes can be lysed only when the antigen is present
at a high density (3.8% molar ratio or higher). At antigen
densities below 0.8% the lysis is no longer observed.
These low density GM1-liposomes (0.8%) can be effi-
ciently lysed by an anti-GM1 serum obtained by repetitive
immunization of a rabbit with GM1 (Panzetta et al.,
1987), indicating that GM1 in low density liposomes is
immunologically accessible (results not shown). Seven
additional normal plasmas showing anti-GM1 IgM immu-
noreactivity by HPTLC immunostaining were tested and
none of them was able to produce the lysis of low density
GM1-liposomes (results not shown).
The failure of normal human plasma to produce the
lysis of low density GM1-liposomes could be due to the
low affinity of the anti-GM1 IgM-antibodies present in
normal individuals (Mizutamari et al., 1994). This possi-
bility was studied taking advantage of two previously known
facts: 1) normal human anti-GM1 antibodies react with the
same affinity with GA1, a glycolipid structurally related to
GM1, and 2) the presence in normal human plasma of an
additional population of high affinity IgM-antibodies which
recognizes GA1 speciﬁcally (it does not cross-react with
GM1).As these populations of antibodies can be distinguished
by their cross-reactivity with GM1, for their separation normal
human plasma was passed through a GM1 affinity column
and the retained antibodies were eluted with potassium
thiocyanate as described in Materials and Methods. Both
the retained and the non-retained antibodies were charac-
terized by HPTLC immunostaining and ELISA. Figure
2A shows that the non-retained (NR) fraction contains
non-cross-reacting anti-GA1 antibodies and non-related
anti-GM2 antibodies but is free of the anti-GM1/GD1b
immunoreactivity. The retained (R) fraction contains
antibodies that react with GM1/GD1b but also with GA1.
ELISA determinations (Fig. 2B) indicate an anti-GA1
titer 3 times higher for NR compared to R fractions (NR:
7.4, R: 2.5). The affinities of the antibody populations for
GA1 were estimated by the potassium thiocyanate crite-
rion (Shimizu et al., 1978; Mizutamari et al., 1994). In
Figure 2C, it can be seen that the NR fraction is composed
mainly of antibodies whose binding is resistant to wash-
ing with 1 M potassium thiocyanate (high affinity antibod-
ies), while most of the antibodies of the R fraction were
washed out in this condition. Both populations of antibod-
ies were assayed for complement-dependent lysis using
Fig. 1. Complement-mediated lysis of GM1-liposomes by
normal human plasma. Ten microliter aliquots of plasma were
assayed for lysis capability toward cholesterol-phosphatidyl
choline liposomes containing 13.6 (A), 3.8 (B), 0.8 (C), and
0.16 (D) molar percent of GM1. Lysis was monitored by the
liberation of umbelliferyl-phosphate and quantiﬁed using alka-
line phosphatase and ﬂuorescence measuring of umbelliferone.
Results are expressed as percent of lysis considering as 100%
the lysis obtained in the presence of 0.1% Triton X-100. The
liposome lysis in C and D is in the order of spontaneous lysis
(ﬂuorescence release in the absence of antibodies).
Affinity of Anti-GM1 IgM-Antibodies
high (3.8%) and low (0.8%) density GA1-liposomes. The
titer differences between both fractions were corrected by
using a 3 times higher aliquot for the R fraction.As can be
seen in Figure 3A, when low density liposomes were used, the
different fractions showed a clear difference in their lysis
capability: while the NR fraction behaved similar to
plasma, the lysis produced by the R fraction was very
low. With high density liposomes, although differences
between both populations were found, the R fraction
showed a remarkable capability to produce the liposome
lysis (Fig. 3B), indicating that low affinity antibodies are
able to ﬁx complement only when they interact with
liposomes that contain antigen in a high density.
In contrast to the complete absence in normal
individuals (Mizutamari et al., 1994), in the plasma of a
patient with sensory polyneuropathy we found high
affinity anti-GM1 binding of IgM-antibodies (Fig. 4). In
this patient, total anti-GM1 titer was slightly higher than
normal and no IgGs were detected (results not shown). In
agreement with the results shown previously, the plasma
of this patient can produce the lysis of low density
Normal human plasma can produce complement-
mediated lysis of GM1-containing liposomes in a density-
dependent manner: the lysis is not observed below a
certain value of antigen density. This phenomenon had
already been seen with a tumor-speciﬁc anti-glycolipid
IgM monoclonal antibody (Nores et al., 1987). This can
Fig. 2. Isolation and characterization of anti-GA1 antibody
populations from normal human plasma. Five milliliters of plasma
was passed through a GM1 affinity column. The retained fraction
was eluted with 1 M potassium thiocyanate, desalted, and
diluted to an equal volume of the original plasma. A: HPTLC
immunostaining of a battery of different gangliosides by 1/10
dilutions of plasma (P), the non-retained (NR) fraction, and the
retained (R) fraction. B: ELISAof the retained (X) and non-retained
(W) fractions. Fifty microliters of 2-fold serial dilutions of each
fraction was incubated in microtiter plate wells coated with 25
pmol of GA1. Antibody binding was detected using peroxidase-
conjugated rabbit anti-human IgM-antibodies. Arrows indicate
the VP0.5 values for each curve. Inset: Double reciprocal plot of
the values. For details see Materials and Methods. The values
are the mean of 3 determinations. C: Affinity estimation of the
antibodies present in the retained (R) and the non-retained (NR)
fraction. Antibody binding to GA1 of a ﬁxed dilution (1/10) of
both fractions was measured by ELISA. Before adding the
secondary antibody, a 5 min washing step with 1 M (closed
bars) or 3 M (open bars) potassium thiocyanate was included.
Controls (hatched bars) were treated with PBS. The values are
Fig. 3. Complement-mediated lysis of low and high density
GA1-liposomes by anti-GA1 antibody populations. Liposomes
containing 0.8% (A) or 3.8% (B) of GA1 were incubated with
the human plasma fractions retained (W) and non-retained (M)
by the GM1 affinity column. Ten microliters of the non-retained
fraction was used as assay aliquots. To correct the titer
difference with the non-retained fraction an aliquot 3 times
higher (30 µl) of the retained fraction was assayed.
be understood if it is considered that anti-ganglioside
antibodies present in normal human plasma are of the
IgM isotype (Mizutamari et al., 1994) and that this type of
immunoglobulins is multivalent. A high antigen density
allows multiple interactions and consequently an increase
in the avidity. For low affinity antibodies (as for normal
plasma anti-GM1) the possibility of having high avidity
through multiple interactions will be important to get
antigen-antibody complexes with a half-life long enough
to start the complement ﬁxation mechanism. Results
obtained with GA1-liposomes support this hypothesis.
Anti-GM1 antibodies also bound to GA1 with low affinity
(Mizutamari et al., 1994) and, as expected, they are not
able to lyse low density GA1-liposomes. In contrast, these
liposomes can be efficiently lysed by other populations of
normal human anti-GA1 IgM-antibodies, formed mainly
by high affinity antibodies. When high density liposomes
were used, both populations were able to produce the
lysis of the liposomes, indicating that the low affinity
anti-GM1 antibodies have the intrinsic property to ﬁx
The ganglioside GM1, the ganglioside antigen most
frequently recognized by normal and pathological antibod-
ies, is highly concentrated in neurons and myelin (Yu and
Saito, 1988). Although direct measurements are lacking,
if its concentration in tissues is considered, its density in
the cell membrane would be below 1% (lipid molar ratio),
a value comparable to that in our low density liposomes.
Thus, normal human anti-GM1 antibodies should not be
considered harmful, because although they can be de-
tected by solid phase assay, their low affinity would make
them unsuitable to trigger the complement cascade.
According to this, we can expect that the anti-ganglioside
antibodies associated with pathological states should be
high affinity antibodies. Here, the presence of high
affinity anti-GM1 IgM-antibodies was associated with the
capacity of the plasma to produce the lysis of low density
GM1-liposomes.Although no studies of affinity of disease-
associated anti-ganglioside antibodies have been done,
Endo et al. (1984) detected a high incidence of low
density GM1-liposome reactive plasmas (plasmas that
produce more than 10% lysis in 1 hr) in patients with
multiple sclerosis, systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE),
or traumatic and ischemic brain damage.
According to our results, consideration should be
made in relation to the utility of the anti-ganglioside
antibody titer measures. The value of titer is dependent on
the amount and the affinity of the antibodies present in the
serum. A serum with a relatively large amount of low
affinity antibodies could have a similar titer to that of a
serum with a relatively small amount of high affinity
antibodies. Considering that only the high affinity antibod-
ies could be pathologically relevant, the measurement of
total antibodies without any distinction between high and
low affinity populations can hide important information.
We consider that the inclusion of a washing step with 1 M
KSCN (to disbound the low affinity antibodies) will make
the methods of antibody binding measurements more
appropriate to study the association between disease and
It can be speculated that anti-GM1 pathological
antibodies can originate from normal antibodies by
changes in the affinity by somatic mutation, i.e., during
the immune response to antigens that mimic GM1 ganglio-
side. In agreement with this possibility is the ﬁnding of
Paterson et al. (1995) that anti-GM1 antibodies cloned
from motor neuropathy patients show extensive somatic
mutations in the V genes. Finally, affinity differences
would also explain why some patients with anti-GM1
